title: Searching Algorithms in Java
summary: Binary search intuition, boundary variants (lower/upper bound), rotated arrays, and when NOT to binary search.

questions.prompt: Binary search time complexity on a sorted array of n elements:
questions.explanation: Each step discards half the remaining elements — log₂n steps maximum.

questions.prompt: Why write m = l + (r - l) / 2 instead of m = (l + r) / 2?
choices: Prevents integer overflow when l and r are large
It rounds up instead of down
questions.explanation: l + r can exceed Integer.MAX_VALUE when both are large; l + (r-l)/2 stays safe.

questions.prompt: Binary search loop condition l <= r vs l < r:
choices: l <= r: classic exact-match search; l < r: boundary/lower-bound variants
questions.explanation: Classic search exits when element found or space empty (l > r). Boundary variants exit when l == r — the answer.

questions.prompt: Binary search on [1,3,5,7,9], target=6. Returns:
questions.explanation: 6 is not in the array. l crosses r without finding it → return -1.

questions.prompt: Lower bound returns:
choices: The FIRST index where nums[i] >= target
The LAST index where nums[i] == target
questions.explanation: Visualise array split: [< target | >= target]. Lower bound finds the left edge of the right region.

questions.prompt: Upper bound returns:
choices: The first index where nums[i] > target
questions.explanation: Visualise: [<= target | > target]. Upper bound finds the left edge of the strictly-greater region.

questions.prompt: To count occurrences of target in a sorted array:
questions.explanation: The difference of both bounds gives the count in O(log n).

questions.prompt: In a rotated sorted array [4,5,6,1,2,3], you compare nums[l] and nums[m] to:
choices: Determine which half is sorted so you can decide where to search
questions.explanation: If nums[l] <= nums[m] the left half is sorted; otherwise the right half is sorted.

questions.prompt: Search for 1 in [4,5,6,1,2,3]. Which half does the algorithm search first?
questions.explanation: Left half is sorted [4,5,6]; 1 is not in it, so search moves to the right half.

questions.prompt: Binary search requires the input to be:
choices: Monotonically ordered — sorted ascending OR the predicate is monotonic
questions.explanation: Binary search generalises to any monotonic predicate — not just sorted arrays.

questions.prompt: Binary search on the answer space means:
choices: Searching inside the array for the answer
Applying binary search over the RANGE of possible answers using a feasibility predicate
questions.explanation: If feasibility(x) is monotonic (false…false…true…true), binary search finds the transition.

questions.prompt: Find square root of n (integer) using binary search. The answer space is:
questions.explanation: lo=0, hi=n. Predicate: is mid*mid <= n? Largest mid satisfying it is floor(√n).

questions.prompt: In lower-bound, the loop condition is l < r (not l <= r) because:
choices: Loop exits when l == r pointing to the answer — no equality check needed mid-loop
questions.explanation: When l == r both pointers are at the same answer location — no need to keep narrowing.

questions.prompt: Which of these are valid binary search applications?
choices: Find first bad version in a sequence
Find peak element in a unimodal array
Find median of two sorted arrays
Find maximum in an unsorted array
questions.explanation: Unsorted max requires O(n) linear scan. The others have a monotonic or halving structure.

questions.prompt: Setting r = m (not r = m-1) in lower-bound is necessary because:
choices: m might BE the answer — discarding it would lose it
questions.explanation: In boundary variants you can't discard m because it may be the leftmost valid index.

questions.prompt: lower_bound([1,2,2,2,3], target=2) returns:
questions.explanation: Index 1 is the first position where nums[i] >= 2.

questions.prompt: Rotated sorted array [3,4,5,1,2]: nums[l]=3, nums[m]=5, nums[r]=2. Which half is sorted?
questions.explanation: nums[l]=3 <= nums[m]=5 → left half [3,4,5] is sorted.

questions.prompt: Why does binary search on 'minimum days to make m bouquets' work?
choices: feasibility(days) is monotonic — more days always makes it easier
questions.explanation: Classic answer-space binary search: predicate transitions from false to true at one threshold.

questions.prompt: Binary search iterations to find target in array of size 1 000 000:
questions.explanation: log₂(1 000 000) ≈ 19.9 → at most 20 iterations.

questions.prompt: When binary search returns l after a lower-bound search:
choices: l is always the target index
l is the insertion point — where target would be inserted to keep array sorted
l could be past the array end
Both B and C are correct
questions.explanation: l == nums.length if target is larger than all elements — always bounds-check before accessing nums[l].

flashcards.back: O(log n) — halves the search space each step.
m = l + (r - l) / 2  — never overflows unlike (l+r)/2.
Classic: l<=r, return m on hit. Boundary: l<r, r=m (keep candidate), exit when l==r.
First index where nums[i] >= target. Splits array: [< target | >= target].
First index where nums[i] > target. Count of target = upper - lower.
One half is ALWAYS sorted. Compare nums[l] vs nums[m] to identify which one.
Define feasibility predicate. If it is monotonic, binary search finds the tipping point.
Ask: 'Can I discard m?' If m could be the answer, use r=m not r=m-1.

project.brief:
Write SearchingLab.java with:
1. binarySearch(int[] nums, int target) → int  (classic)
2. lowerBound(int[] nums, int target) → int
3. upperBound(int[] nums, int target) → int
4. searchRotated(int[] nums, int target) → int
5. minCapacityToShip(int[] weights, int days) → int  (answer-space binary search)
Include main() tests covering: target absent, all-duplicates, single element, rotated at index 0.

checklist.text: lowerBound returns nums.length when target > all elements
upperBound - lowerBound gives correct count of duplicates
searchRotated handles rotation at every position including index 0
minCapacityToShip finds the exact minimum — not just any valid capacity

hints: lowerBound: loop is l < r, update r = m (not m-1) when nums[m] >= target.
searchRotated: the condition nums[l] <= nums[m] correctly identifies the sorted left half.
minCapacityToShip: lo = max(weights) (must carry heaviest), hi = sum(weights) (one day).
